body:
UNSW Business School
INFS2822 Programming for Data Analytics




Lecture 3
Python Fundamentals Part III

Term 3, 2025
  Copyright
• There are some file-sharing websites that specialise in buying and selling
 academic work to and from university students.

• If you upload your original work to these websites, and if another student
 downloads and presents it as their own either wholly or partially, you
 might be found guilty of collusion — even years after graduation.

• These file-sharing websites may also accept purchase of course
 materials, such as copies of lecture slides and tutorial handouts. By law,
 the copyright on course materials, developed by UNSW staff in the
 course of their employment, belongs to UNSW. It constitutes copyright
 infringement, if not academic misconduct, to trade these materials.
 Country

  leadership and contributions,

Individual and Team Assignment
• 5%: One (1) Individual Assignment (Release in Week 3 Monday)
  ⎻ Turnitin submission of .docx file
  ⎻ Assignment description available on Moodle
  ⎻ Submission by Week 5 Friday 17th October, 3 pm AEST

• 30% Team Assignment (Release in Week 5 Monday)
  ⎻ Lab 10: Team Assignment Presentation in person on campus
  ⎻ Assignment description available on Moodle
  ⎻ Submission, including code in .ipynb, PPT, report, by Week 10
    Friday 21th November 2024, 3 pm AEST
                8
Outline
• Tuple

• List

• Set

• Dictionary
                                                                              9
Tuple
• A tuple is a sequence of values separated by commas enclosed by a pair
 of parentheses ( )
   ⎻ The values stored in a tuple can be any type, and they are indexed by
     integers
   ⎻ Tuples are used to store multiple items in a single variable.
      my_tuple = ("country music", 5, 3.65)
                 # music style, music typeID, avg length
      my_tuple


• Check the type of the tuple
      type(my_tuple)
                                                                10
Indexing Tuple Elements
• A tuple may have many elements
• Each element can be accessed via an index
• Relationship between the index and the items in the tuple
        my_tuple = ("country music", 5, 3.65)

                                         print(my_tuple[0])
Index     Index   Element                print(my_tuple[1])
0         -3      "country music"        print(my_tuple[2])
1         -2      5
                                         print(my_tuple[-3])
2         -1      3.65
                                         print(my_tuple[-2])
                                         print(my_tuple[-1])
                                                          11
Slicing Tuples
• We can slice a tuple to obtain new tuples with the
 corresponding elements

• We can obtain the first N elements of a tuple
     tuple1 = ("country music", 5, 3.65, 25010, "yes")
     tuple1[0:3] # first 3 elements


• We can obtain the last N elements of a tuple
     tuple1[-3:] # last 3 elements
                                                                  12
Tuples Are Immutable
We cannot change the elements of the tuple or the tuple itself
once it’s created

 # an example of immutable
 Creating a tuple
 my_tuple = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)

 # Trying to change a value in the tuple
 my_tuple[0] = 10 # what happens?
                                                          13
Tuple Concatenation Using +
tuple1 = ("country music", 5, 3.65)
tuple2 = tuple1 + (25010, "yes") # total music
count, availability
tuple2

                       Index   Index   Element
                       0       -5      "country music"
                       1       -4      5
                       2       -3      3.65
                       3       -2      25010
                       4       -1      "yes"
                                                                         14
Nested Tuple
• A tuple can contain another tuple as well as other data types. This
  process is called “nesting”
• Consider the following tuple with nesting:
 tuple_nested = ( ("country music", "country and
 western") , 5, 3.65)
• In a nested tuple, an element can be a tuple
     Index          Element
     0              ("country music", "country and western")
     1              5
     2              3.65
                                                                              15
Accessing Elements in Nested Tuple
We can use the second index to access the 2nd level elements
tuple_nested = ( ("country music", "country and western") , 10, 3.5)

                     Index        Element
                     0            ("country music", "country and western")
                     1            10
                     2            3.5




                                             tuple_nested[0][0]

                                                        tuple_nested[0][1]
                                                                          16
  Example: Employee Record
  Imagine a human resources system where employee records
  are stored. Each employee record might include the employee
  ID, name, role, and hire date

# Defining an employee record as a tuple
employee_record = (101, "John Doe", "Software Engineer", "2023-09-26")

# Accessing elements in the tuple
employee_id = employee_record[0] # 101
employee_name = employee_record[1] # "John Doe"
employee_role = employee_record[2] # "Software Engineer"
hire_date = employee_record[3] # "2023-09-26"
                                                                          17
Summary
• A tuple is used to store multiple data items in a single variable
• A tuple is written with a pair of parentheses ( )
• A tuple is a collection of data items which is immutable

                     Mutable    Ordered      Indexing   Duplicate Data


       Tuple            ×
                18
Outline
• Tuple

• List

• Set

• Dictionary
                                                                          19
List
• A list is a collection of elements or items (e.g., strings, numbers)
 enclosed by a pair of square brackets [ ] with each element
 separated by a comma
     months = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar']
• A list can contain other Python objects, e.g., a tuple
      mixed = ['spam', 2.0, 5, (10, 20)]
• A list can be empty
       mixed = []
• Print out the list directly will print each element one by one
      months = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar']
      print(months) # print the list directly
                                                20
Create a List

• Method 1: use the built-in function list()
     fruits = list()



• Method 2: use a pair of square brackets
     fruits = [] # more common
                                                                   21
Positive Indexing in a List
• The address of each element within a list is called an index.
  An index is used to access and refer to an item within a list
• Elements are indexed in list
months = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar']
print(months[0])
print(months[1])
print(months[2])       Index                    Element
                             0                  "Jan"
                             1                  "Feb"
                             2                  "Mar"
                                                               22
Negative Indexing in a List
The index starts from -1 at the end, and decreases as we go
towards the left


Index        Element
                           months = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar']
0 (-3)       "Jan"         print(months[-3])
1 (-2)       "Feb"         print(months[-2])
2 (-1)       "Mar"         print(months[-1])
                                                                     23
List is Mutable/Changeable
• We can change the items in a list or reassign an item in a list
 after the list is formed

• When the index appears on the left side of an assignment, it
 identifies the element of the list that will be changed

      days = ['Mon', 'Tue', 'Wed']
      days[0] = 'Sun'
      print(days)
                                                                                24
Concatenating and Slicing a List
  first = ['Mon','Tue', 'Wed']
  second = ['Thu', 'Fri']
  third = ['Sat', 'Sun']


  days_of_week = first + second + third         #concatenating
  print(days_of_week)
  print(len(days_of_week))
  print(days_of_week[0:3])      #slicing


Note: [0:3] means the index starts with 0, and the ending index is up to but
not including index 3
                                                 25
Exercise
Build a list with the following four elements
      1, hello, [1,2,3,4] and True
my_list = [1, 'hello', [1,2,3,4], True]

What does the below retrieve?

print(my_list[2][3])
print(my_list[1][2])
 Popular Built-in Functions for List                    26

Operations: append(), sort()
fruits = list() # or fruits = [ ]
fruits.append('apple')
#append() can add ONE element to the end of the list

fruits.append('orange')
fruits.append('banana')
print(fruits)

fruits.sort() #sorts the list ascending by default
print(fruits) #what you see? – ASCII table
print(len(fruits))
 Popular Built-in Functions for List                      27

Operations: insert(), extend()
fruits = ['apple', 'orange', 'banana']
fruits.insert(1, 'peach')
#inserts the specified value at the specified position
print(fruits)

fruits = ['apple', 'orange', 'banana']
fruits.extend(['peach', 'pear'])
#extends the current list by adding the specified list
elements to the end of the current list
print(fruits)
 Popular Built-in Functions for List      28

Operations: remove(), pop()
fruits = ['apple', 'orange', 'banana']
fruits.remove('orange')
#removes the specified element
print(fruits)


fruits = ['apple', 'orange', 'banana']
fruits.pop(1)
#pops out the indexed element
print(fruits)
 Popular Built-in Functions for List                              29

Operations: split()
We can convert a string into a list of words using a delimiter

# By default, the delimiter is ' ', an empty space
desc = 'This course introduces various techniques'
word_in_desc = desc.split()
print(word_in_desc)

# Use comma ',' as the delimiter
desc = 'This, course, introduces, various, techniques'
word_in_desc = desc.split(',')
print(word_in_desc)
                                                            30
Summary of List Properties

           Mutable   Ordered   Indexing   Duplicate Data



   Tuple     ×

    List
                                                                          31
Key Differences: Tuple vs List
List                               Tuple
Enclosed by square brackets [ ]    Enclosed by parentheses ( )

Mutable                            Immutable
- Elements can be modified         - Elements cannot be modified
- We can add items to a list       - We cannot add items to a tuple
- We can remove items in a list    - We cannot remove items in a tuple

More functions can be performed on Less functions can be performed on
a list                             a tuple
Less memory efficient              More memory efficient
 32
                33
Outline
• Tuple

• List

• Set

• Dictionary
                                                                          34
Set
• A set is a collection of unordered unique objects in Python. You can
  denote a set with a pair of curly brackets { }
    ⎻ Objects can be of different data types
• Python will automatically remove duplicate items in a set when you
  create it
  set1 = {"pop", "rock", "rock", "R&B", "rock", "country music"}
  set1
• What really happened?
            {"pop", "rock", "rock", "R&B", "rock", "country music"}




                    {"pop", "rock", "R&B", "country music"}
                                                               35
Create a Set from a List

album_list = [ "pop", "rock", "rock", "R&B", "rock" ]
album_set = set(album_list) #set() converts a list to a set
album_set




                                 What is the printout?
                                 {'R&B', 'pop', 'rock'}
 Built-in Functions for Set Operations:                36

add()
album_list = {"pop", "rock", "rock", "R&B", "rock"}
album_list.add("country music")
#adds the specified element
print(album_list)
 Built-in Functions for Set Operations:                37

remove(), discard()
album_list = {"pop", "rock", "rock", "R&B", "rock"}
album_list.remove("pop")
#removes the specified element
print(album_list)


album_list = {"pop", "rock", "rock", "R&B", "rock"}
album_list.discard("pop")
#discards the specified element
print(album_list)
                                                           38
Set Operations
• add() method: add an element to a set
• remove() method: remove an item from a set
• in command: verify if an element is in the set
  A = set(["Yesterday once more", "Five hundred miles",
  "Paradise"])
  print(A)

  A.add("My all in all")
  print(A)

  A.remove("My all in all")
  print(A)

  "My all in all" in A
                                                           39
Set Operations: Union
• union() method
• operator |
 A = {'red', 'green', 'blue'}
 B = {'yellow', 'red', 'orange'}

 # by operator
 print(A | B)                      green         orange
                                           red
 # by method
 print(A.union(B))                 blue          yellow
                                                            40
Set Operations: Intersection
• intersection() method
• operator &
  A = {'red', 'green', 'blue'}
  B = {'yellow', 'red', 'orange'}

  # by operator
  print(A & B)                      green         orange
                                            red
  # by method
                                    blue
  print(A.intersection(B))                        yellow
                                                             41
Set Operations: Difference
• difference() method
• operator -
   A = {'red', 'green', 'blue'}
   B = {'yellow', 'red', 'orange'}

   # by operator
   print(A - B)
                                     green         orange
   # by method                               red
   print(A.difference(B))
                                     blue          yellow
   print(B.difference(A))
                                                            42
Set Operations: Symmetric Difference
• symmetric_difference() method
• operator ^
 A = {'red', 'green', 'blue'}
 B = {'yellow', 'red', 'orange'}

 # by operator
 print(A ^ B)                                     orange
                                    green
                                            red
 # by method
 print(A.symmetric_difference(B))   blue          yellow
                                                           43
Exercise: Set operations
A = {0, 2, 4, 6, 8}
B = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}

# union
                                  What is the printout?
print("Union :", A | B)
# intersection
print("Intersection :", A & B)
# difference
print("Difference :", A - B)
# symmetric difference
print("Symmetric difference :", A ^ B)
                                                                            44
Summary of Set Properties
                    Mutable     Ordered       Indexing    Duplicate Data

   Tuple              ×
    List
    Set                            ×             ×              ×
  • Mutable: Elements can be changed
  • Unordered: Items stored in a set aren’t kept in any particular order
  • Unindexed: You cannot access set items by referring to an index
  • No duplicate data
                45
Outline
• Tuple

• List

• Set

• Dictionary
                                                                       46
Dictionary
• A dictionary is a mutable, unordered collection that stores
  mappings of unique keys to values. The building blocks are key-
  value pairs
• Values can be of any data type
• Values of the Python dictionary may or may not be unique
• Keys are always unique/only a single element within a dictionary,
  because keys help us locate the data in the dictionary
• Keys must be immutable. Hence, the key can only be strings,
  numbers or tuples

veggie_price = {'green pepper':15, 'tomato':6.9, 'green onion':3.5}
                                                                       47
Inside a Dictionary
• A dictionary holds a sequence of elements

• Each element is a key-value pair
veggie_price = {'green pepper':15, 'tomato':6.9, 'green onion':3.5}

                                Key               Value

                           'green pepper'          15

                              'tomato'             6.9

                            'green onion'          3.5
                                                                    48
Create a Dictionary
• Type the key-value pairs in a pair of curly brackets { }
veggie_price = {'green pepper':15, 'tomato':6.9}
print(veggie_price)



• Start with an empty dictionary dict()
fruit_price = dict()
fruit_price = {} # more common
fruit_price['apple'] = 3.5 # does the key exist previously?
fruit_price['orange'] = 5
fruit_price['blueberry'] = 20
print(fruit_price) #{'apple': 3.5, 'orange': 5, 'blueberry': 20}
                                                                     49
Extracting Data from a Dictionary
• We can refer to a key in a dictionary and extract the value for
 that key: Dictionary_name['key_name']


    veggie_price2 = {'green pepper':15, 'tomato':6.9}
    print(veggie_price2 ['tomato'])
    print(veggie_price2 ['potato'])
    # does the key exist?
 Extracting data from a Dictionary                              50

Using get()
get() returns the value given a key as an argument, and does
not trigger an error if the key is non-existent

   veggie_price2 = {'green pepper':15, 'tomato':6.9}
   print(veggie_price2)

   veggie_price2['tomato'] # what happens?

   veggie_price2.get('potato') # what happens?
                                                                 51
Adding and Changing Elements
How do we differentiate whether we are adding a new key-value
pair, or changing the value of an existing key?

veggie_price2 = {'green pepper':15, 'tomato':6.9}
print(veggie_price2)

veggie_price2['green onion'] = 3.5 # new key-value pair
veggie_price2['tomato'] = 3 # update an existing key’s value
print(veggie_price2)
 Operations for Dictionary:                                                         52

keys(), values(), items()
• Get all the keys in dictionary
       veggie_price2.keys()
• Get all the values in dictionary
       veggie_price2.values()
• items() returns a view object. The view object contains the key-value pairs of
 the dictionary, as a list of tuples
       veggie_price2 = {'green pepper':15, 'tomato':6.9}
       veggie_price_tuple = veggie_price2.items()
       print(veggie_price_tuple)

                        What’s the printout?
 Operations for Dictionary:                                                53

del(), in
• Delete elements (key-value pairs) by the key
      del(veggie_price2['tomato'])


• Checking the existence of a key in a dictionary using the in operator
veggie_price2 = {'green pepper':15, 'tomato':6.9}
print('tomato' in veggie_price2.keys())
                                                                                                                                                            54
Summary of Dictionary Property

                                                                                                                               Duplicate
                                              Mutable                   Ordered                    Indexing
                                                                                                                                 Data

              Tuple                                  ×
                List

                Set                                                            ×                           ×                            ×
         Dictionary                                                                 1                          1
                                                                                                                                        ×
1 Since Python 3.7, dictionaries are order-preserving, that is they now behave like collections OrderedDicts. There is no dedicated method to index into
keys and values of the dictionary
                                                                             55
Built-in Function: sorted()
• sorted() function takes a given tuple, list, string, set, or dictionary
 as a parameter and return a sorted list with the same elements in
 ascending or descending order

• E.g., sort a tuple of scores and save the sorted result to a new list
         scores = (0, 3, 2, 5, 10, 8, 9)
         scores_sorted = sorted(scores)
         scores_sorted # [0, 2, 3, 5, 8, 9, 10]

• How do we sort the tuple in descending order?
        scores = (0, 3, 2, 5, 10, 8, 9)
        scores_sorted = sorted(scores, reverse=True)
        scores_sorted # [10, 9, 8, 5, 3, 2, 0]
                                                           56
Recap Exercise
• What happens if we sort the dictionary below?
• What are we sorting by?
• What does the sorted result contain? Key-value pairs?

name_scores = {'John':15, 'Mary':69, 'Atul':63}
sorted_list = sorted(name_scores)
sorted_list
             57
Questions
